summary: I was born in Iowa but only stayed there during infancy, so I have no real recollection of it. My father was a professor at Penn College in Oskaloosa, Iowa, which was a Quaker college. Although I left Iowa early, it was the starting point of my family's educational and professional journey.

My father taught at Penn College, a Quaker institution in Oskaloosa, Iowa, before he went to Germany for further studies. Several Quaker families in San Jose, including ours, had connections to Penn College, which influenced our move here. It was an important part of my father's early career and shaped our family's network.

Oskaloosa, Iowa, was where my father worked as a professor at Penn College before he pursued studies in Germany. Though I was born in Iowa, I don't remember Oskaloosa personally since we left when I was very young. Still, it was the place where my father's academic career began.

My father spent about three years studying at the University of Bonn in Germany after his time at Penn College. This period was part of his advanced education before he returned to the US to continue his career in education. His time in Bonn was significant in shaping his academic background.

Alongside Bonn, my father also studied at the University of Münster during his three years in Germany. This was part of his preparation before returning to take on leadership roles in education back in the States. His time there contributed to his qualifications and expertise.

After returning from Germany, my father became the principal of the high school in Colorado Springs. This was a key step in his educational career before he moved on to become president of the University of Nevada. Our family moved around a bit due to his various positions.

My father served as president of the University of Nevada in the late 1880s and early 1890s. This was a prominent role in his career before our family eventually settled in San Jose. His leadership there was an important part of his professional life.

Our family moved to San Jose because there were many Quaker families here, some of whom had connections to Penn College where my father taught. From grammar school through high school and college, I have lived in San Jose ever since. It was also the place where I witnessed significant political upheaval during my youth.

Santa Clara High School became a focal point of political protest when about 150 students, including my brother, left San Jose High School to attend there. This migration was sparked by the firing of a popular Latin teacher and was part of a larger political struggle in San Jose. It was a significant event during my high school years.

I graduated from San Jose High School, where my brother also studied before joining the protest migration to Santa Clara High School. The firing of Professor L. R. Smith from San Jose High was a political move that caused unrest among students. My time there coincided with a politically charged atmosphere in the city.

Johnny McKansia was one of the political leaders in San Jose during my youth, alongside J. Ray. He was a figure of controversy, especially during the time when students protested the firing of a popular teacher. The boys even chanted about him during their march to Santa Clara High School, showing his prominence in local politics.

J. Ray was another key political leader in San Jose at the same time as Johnny McKansia. Together, they led the political organization that was challenged by local reform movements. Their leadership was central to the political dynamics I witnessed growing up here.

Professor L. R. Smith was a Latin teacher at San Jose High School who was very popular among students. His firing by the Board of Education was seen as a political move and sparked a student protest that led many to transfer to Santa Clara High School. His dismissal was a catalyst for political activism in the community.

The Good Government League was organized in our county as a reform movement led by J. O. Rayce and K. A. Sayre, the Hayes brothers. My father was a member and delegate to the 1906 Republican National Convention, reflecting his involvement in this movement. I was impressed by their efforts to improve local politics during a time of upheaval.

J. O. Rayce, along with K. A. Sayre, led the Good Government League in our county. Their leadership was instrumental in pushing for political reform during a turbulent time in San Jose. I admired their dedication to good governance and was influenced by their movement.

K. A. Sayre was one of the Hayes brothers who led the Good Government League alongside J. O. Rayce. Their efforts to promote honest and efficient government made a significant impression on me during my youth. They represented the reform spirit that was growing in our community.

A few years after the Good Government League formed, the Progressive movement swept across the nation, including here in California. This movement aligned with the ideals of reform and government accountability that I had seen locally. It was a time of political awakening and change that influenced my views.

My father was a Republican and involved in the party's activities, including being a delegate to the 1906 Republican National Convention. I grew up in a Republican household and naturally joined the party myself, even serving as president of the Stanford Republican Club. The party was central to my political identity and early involvement in campaigns.

My father was a delegate to the famous Republican National Convention held in Santa Cruz in 1906. This event was a highlight of his political engagement and reflected his idealism within the party. It was part of the broader political landscape I grew up observing.

The McClatchy family, owners of the Sacramento Bee, are a notable example of political loyalty across generations. The founder had a liberal outlook, which his sons C. K. and J. S. McClatchy faithfully continued. Even the third generation maintained the founder's views except for moving away from Henry George's Single Tax theory.

The Sacramento Bee was owned and operated by the McClatchy family, who maintained a consistent liberal editorial stance over generations. The paper reflected the founder's ideals and influenced California politics. It was well known for its progressive views, except for later generations not supporting the Single Tax.

C. K. McClatchy was one of the sons of the Sacramento Bee's founder who loyally carried on his father's liberal policies. His leadership helped maintain the paper's political slant through the second generation. His family continued to influence the paper's direction well after his time.

J. S. McClatchy, alongside his brother C. K., upheld their father's liberal and forward-looking editorial policies at the Sacramento Bee. Together, they ensured the paper remained influential in California politics. Their stewardship was a key part of the paper's history.

Henry George was the economist behind the Single Tax theory, which the original McClatchy founder supported. While later generations of the McClatchy family did not continue to espouse this principle, it was an important part of the founder's political philosophy. His ideas influenced progressive taxation debates.

The Single Tax was a theory advocated by Henry George and supported by the founder of the Sacramento Bee. It aimed at reforming taxation based on land value. Although later McClatchy generations abandoned this principle, it was significant in early progressive thought and influenced political discussions.

The Vendome Hotel was where Mr. Dougherty, a family acquaintance and bookkeeper, worked. He influenced me greatly by encouraging me to pursue law just before I entered college. His advice was pivotal in shaping my career path toward the legal profession.

I attended Stanford University starting the fall after I graduated from high school. While there, I became president of the Republican Club and was active in political campaigns. Stanford was where I solidified my interest in law and politics, and it provided me opportunities to engage with state political figures like James Rolph Jr. and Pardee.

The Republican Party managers at the time capitalized on the enthusiasm of college students like us to generate noise and support at rallies. They organized events in places like San Francisco and San Jose, recruiting hundreds of college boys to create a lively atmosphere. I was involved in these activities during my time at Stanford.

San Francisco was the site of major Republican rallies during my college years, where party managers brought in college students to boost enthusiasm. It was also the political center that influenced local politics in places like San Jose. I traveled there for campaign events and witnessed the political machine's influence firsthand.

The Garden Theatre in San Jose was a venue for summer political rallies where Republican Party managers gathered college students to create excitement. These rallies were part of the campaign efforts I participated in as a member of the Stanford Republican Club. The theatre was a hub of political energy during election seasons.

James Rolph Jr. was a political figure I encountered during my time in the Stanford Republican Club. Though not detailed extensively, he was part of the political scene in California that I was involved with. His presence was part of the broader political environment I experienced.

The Victory Theatre in San Jose was a notable venue where I attended a meeting for Pardee's gubernatorial campaign. I even had the opportunity to speak on the platform representing the Stanford Republican Club. This event marked my first meeting with Pardee, who later became governor.

Franklin Knight Lane was the Democratic candidate running against Pardee for governor during my early political involvement. Although I didn't have much insight into the political machine's control at the time, I remember Lane as part of the election I followed. He represented the opposition party in those contests.

During my youth, elections were contested on a party basis, with candidates running under either the Republican or Democratic banner. The Democratic Party, represented by figures like Franklin Knight Lane, was the opposition to my family's Republican affiliation. I observed the party dynamics closely during the early 1900s political campaigns.
